results.question: How did Romulus die?
results.ideal_answer: The sources disagree. The official version, in both Livy and Plutarch, is that while Romulus was reviewing the army at the Goat's Marsh a sudden storm enveloped him and he was carried up to heaven, thereafter worshipped as the god Quirinus. But both historians also preserve a darker rumor: that the senators (fathers), resentful of his rule, fell upon him and tore his body to pieces, each carrying away a part — Plutarch placing the deed in the temple of Vulcan.

results.question: How did Themistocles die?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides states plainly that disease was the real cause of his death, noting only as a rumor the story that he poisoned himself on finding he could not deliver Greece to the Persian king. Plutarch gives the dramatic suicide as "the usual story" — that Themistocles sacrificed, feasted with his friends, and drank bull's blood — while acknowledging the alternative that it was a fast-acting poison.

results.question: Did Caligula actually intend to make his horse consul?
results.ideal_answer: The two sources differ in confidence. Suetonius reports it only as hearsay — "it is even said that he intended to make him consul." Cassius Dio states it more strongly: that Caligula promised the consulship to the horse Incitatus and "would certainly have done" it had he lived longer. So the claim is a guarded rumor in Suetonius and a near-assertion in Dio.
results.notes: Two versions (hedged rumor vs near-assertion), one attestation each — unlabelled, so both are independently required (singleton default).

results.question: How did Bucephalus die?
results.ideal_answer: The sources disagree on whether the horse died of battle wounds or of old age. Arrian insists explicitly that Bucephalas died "not from having been wounded by any one, but from the effects of toil and old age." Plutarch reports both: that most authorities say he died under treatment for his wounds after the battle with Porus, though Onesicritus says it was fatigue and age. Both agree he was about thirty and that Alexander founded a city (Bucephala) in his memory.

results.question: How did Xerxes get back to Asia after Salamis?
results.ideal_answer: Herodotus himself records two conflicting accounts. In his main narrative, Xerxes marched his army by land to the Hellespont and crossed to Abydos by ship, the bridges having been broken by a storm. But he then reports "another story": that Xerxes sailed from Eion on the Strymon in a Phoenician ship caught in a storm, and that the Persian nobles on deck leapt into the sea to lighten it and save the king — a tale Herodotus relates but disbelieves.
results.notes: The first two spans are within one work, but Herodotus explicitly attributes them as rival accounts — the doc allows clearly-separated passages in one work. Rawlinson is a cross-book echo of the same dispute. GROUPING JUDGMENT (review): Rawlinson references the destroyed bridge (land) and the ship "according to some" (sea), so it is tagged [land, sea]. Narrow it to [sea] if you judge it attests only the ship version.

results.question: How did Remus die?
results.ideal_answer: Livy gives two versions side by side: that Remus fell in the brawl that broke out when the brothers' rival bird-omens were disputed, or — the "more common account" — that he leaped over Romulus's newly-built walls in derision and Romulus killed him in a rage, crying "So shall every one fare, who shall dare to leap over my fortifications." Smith's history follows the leaping-the-wall version as the standard one.

results.question: How did Alexander deal with the Gordian knot?
results.ideal_answer: The sources split on whether he cut it or untied it by a trick. Arrian reports that some say Alexander, unable to loosen the cord, struck it with his sword and cut it through — but that Aristobulus says he pulled out the pin of the wagon-pole and so drew out the yoke. Plutarch gives the same two versions: most authors say he cut it with his sword, but Aristobulus says he undid it easily by pulling out the pin.
results.notes: Both passages give BOTH versions (sword vs. pin), so each is tagged [cut, untie]: retrieving either Arrian or Plutarch covers the whole contradiction. Without grouping, per-span recall would wrongly demand both.

results.question: What did Nero say about an artist dying, and when?
results.ideal_answer: The wording and timing differ between sources. Suetonius reports that as his grave was dug before his eyes Nero wept and kept repeating "What an artist is now about to perish!" Cassius Dio gives a different form and moment: that Nero spoke the "far-famed sentence" — "Jupiter, what an artist perishes in me!" — at the very instant he killed himself.
results.notes: Two versions (wording/timing), one attestation each — unlabelled, so both are independently required (singleton default).

results.question: Why did Tarpeia betray the citadel, and how did she die?
results.ideal_answer: All sources agree she was bribed by the gold the Sabines wore on their left arms and was crushed to death under their shields, but they differ on her intent. Livy gives the standard treacherous version (she was bribed to admit the soldiers and crushed under their arms) but also reports an alternative: that she demanded their shields in good faith, meaning to disarm them, and was killed as if a traitor by the very reward she chose. Plutarch agrees she coveted the golden bracelets and asked for "the things they wore on their left arms."

results.question: What was Lycurgus' date and life story?
results.ideal_answer: The sources cannot agree on almost anything about him. Plutarch opens his Life by warning that scarcely anything asserted by one historian is not contradicted by another — as to his family, his travels, the manner of his death, and above all the very age in which he lived. Grote sharpens the specific disagreement: Herodotus makes Lycurgus uncle and guardian of king Labotas of the Agid line (placing him about 996 BC), whereas all other accounts make him a younger brother of the rival Proclid line.
results.notes: GROUPING JUDGMENT (review): this is a meta-contradiction shading into synthesis. Only the two primary attestations of the Herodotus/Agid-line version (Herodotus I.66 + Pausanias III) are grouped as alternatives [herodotus-version]; Plutarch (the "all sources disagree" thesis), Grote (the specific disagreement framing), and Bury (sharpening) are left as distinct singleton requirements. Adjust if you want a different facet split.

results.question: Who was to blame for losing the battle of Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: Both sources fault the consul Varro (Gaius Terentius), but with different emphasis. Polybius lays the disaster squarely on him personally — his flight to Venusia was "as disgraceful to himself as his conduct in office had been disastrous to his country." Mommsen, while equally scornful of "the hero of the pavement," frames the deeper fault as systemic: the old custom of alternating supreme command between the two consuls day by day, which forced the cautious Paullus to yield to Varro's will.

results.question: How was the city of Nineveh captured and the Assyrian empire brought to an end?
results.ideal_answer: The accounts disagree on how Nineveh fell. In the version Herodotus and Rawlinson follow, it was taken by a Median assault: Cyaxares, allied with the Babylonian Nabopolassar, pressed the siege until the Assyrian king's means of resistance were exhausted and he burned himself in his palace. A rival tradition, reported from Ctesias, has the walls breached not by storm but by nature — an unusually wet season swelled the Tigris until it burst in and swept away more than two miles of wall, after which the king set fire to himself. Both traditions end with the king's self-immolation, but differ on whether the city fell to siege or to flood.
results.notes: Genuine ancient dispute: the standard Median-siege account vs the Ctesias/Diodorus river-flood legend. Rawlinson relays the flood version as Ctesias's while doubting it; its primary source (Diodorus Bk II) is outside the corpus, so Rawlinson Media #16163 is the in-corpus attestation. Fills Rawlinson Assyria #16162 / Media #16163 (were ZERO).

results.question: How large was the army that Xerxes led against Greece?
results.ideal_answer: Herodotus gives an enormous figure: the land army alone amounted to one hundred and seventy myriads (1,700,000), a total he says was reached by herding the troops in ten-thousand-man batches into a walled enclosure and counting how many times it filled. Rawlinson rejects the number as untestable and almost certainly grossly exaggerated — armies, he notes, are never really counted, and so crude a method invited inflation — though he allows the host probably did exceed a million.
results.notes: The ancient total vs the standard critical rejection; both describe the same enclosure-counting method. Routes Rawlinson Persia #16165.

results.question: How did Hannibal die when he was cornered at the court of King Prusias?
results.ideal_answer: The common account is that Hannibal, finding the house surrounded by Prusias's soldiers (sent at the demand of the Roman envoy Flamininus), took poison he had long kept ready and drank it; Livy and Appian both give this poison version. Plutarch, however, records rival traditions about the manner of his death: some said he strangled himself by twisting his cloak about his neck with a servant's help, and others that he drank bull's blood.
results.notes: Sources agree Rome (Flamininus) cornered him; they disagree on the manner of death — poison (Livy, Appian) vs Plutarch's strangling / bull's-blood variants. Fills Livy 37-end #44318 + Appian #900003.

results.question: How large was Hannibal's army when he descended into Italy after crossing the Alps?
results.ideal_answer: The sources do not agree. Polybius gives a precise, low figure — twelve thousand Libyan and eight thousand Iberian infantry and not more than six thousand cavalry — and says he took it from a bronze tablet Hannibal himself set up at the Lacinian promontory. Livy reports that the number is by no means agreed upon by authors: the highest estimates run to a hundred thousand foot and twenty thousand horse, the lowest to twenty thousand foot and six thousand horse (this last agreeing with Polybius).
results.notes: REFRAMED from the candidate's "by which route did Hannibal cross the Alps." The route disagreement is a modern geographic reconstruction — the ancient texts state no rival routes, and Polybius's polemic (III.47-48) targets unnamed writers not in the corpus. The army-size figures ARE a genuine in-corpus contradiction (Polybius's Lacinian-inscription count vs the wide disagreement Livy documents). FLAG FOR REVIEW: accept the reframe or drop.

results.question: How many Romans were killed at the battle of Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: Polybius puts the Roman dead at about seventy thousand — nearly the whole army, since of the infantry actually engaged only some three thousand escaped. Livy gives a smaller toll: forty thousand foot and two thousand seven hundred horse slain, with an equal number of allies among them.
results.notes: Genuine divergence in the casualty figures (~70,000 vs ~40,000). Deepens Livy 9-26 #10907.

results.question: What was the real cause of the Second Punic War?
results.ideal_answer: The sources frame the war's origin differently. Polybius insists that the siege of Saguntum and the crossing of the Ebro were only the first acts of the war, not its causes, and faults the historians who stop there; the true causes, he argues, lay deeper. Livy grounds the war in Saguntum and in Hannibal's inherited enmity — the boy's altar-oath to be an enemy of Rome, and Hamilcar's lasting grievance over the loss of Sicily and Sardinia.
results.notes: Polybius explicitly disputes other historians' causal account (cause vs first-action/pretext), the very framing Livy's narrative exemplifies. Leans analytical but qualifies as a contradiction because Polybius names and rejects the rival view. Deepens Livy 9-26 #10907.

results.question: Who won the battle of Mantinea, and what did it settle?
results.ideal_answer: The verdict is disputed. Xenophon ends his history on a deliberately bleak note: both sides set up trophies and claimed victory, yet neither gained any territory or advantage, and uncertainty and confusion in Greece were tenfold greater after the battle than before — it settled nothing. Diodorus, by contrast, counts it a victory for the Boeotians (Thebans), the verdict the dying Epaminondas is given before he allows the spear to be drawn from his body.
results.notes: Genuine historiographical contradiction — Xenophon's downbeat close (both claimed victory, nothing gained) vs the victory tradition (Diodorus). Fills Xenophon Hellenica #1174 + Diodorus #900005.

results.question: What happened to the captured Athenian generals Nicias and Demosthenes after the Sicilian disaster?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides says the Syracusans put Nicias and Demosthenes to death — butchered against the wishes of the Spartan Gylippus, who had hoped to carry the captured enemy generals home to Sparta. Plutarch preserves a rival account, which he credits to Timaeus: that they were not executed by the Syracusans at all, but, warned by a message from Hermocrates and with the connivance of their guards, were allowed to put an end to themselves.
results.notes: Plutarch (Nicias) explicitly frames the dispute (Thucydides/Philistus: executed by the Syracusans; Timaeus: suicide). Fills Thucydides #7142.

results.question: How do the sources judge Alcibiades?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides judges that his lawless personal extravagance — horses and expenditure beyond his means — made the people fear him as an aspiring tyrant, "and this later on had not a little to do with the ruin of the Athenian state," though his public war conduct was excellent. Plutarch fixes on his chameleon-like versatility, adapting to Spartan austerity or Persian pomp at will. Grote, the modern voice, grants him boldness, resource, and vigor "not surpassed by any contemporary Greek" while damning his want of public principle. Bury, a second modern voice, judges him a born politician but no statesman — lacking the purpose, perseverance, and self-control that statesmanship demands.

results.question: How do the sources judge Themistocles?
results.ideal_answer: Herodotus reports he was esteemed throughout Hellas as "the ablest by far of the Hellenes" and uniquely honored at Sparta. Thucydides' obituary calls his untaught native genius for instant judgment and prediction "quite extraordinary and unparalleled." Grote balances the picture: his rapacity made him hated and feared among the allies, and the suspicions of treasonable medism at Athens grew from his own corrupt and double-faced conduct. Bury, a later modern voice, credits the man of genius who made Athens a sea-power as the greatest of all her statesmen.

results.question: How is the disaster at Cannae described?
results.ideal_answer: Polybius gives the soldier's accounting: of six thousand Roman horse only seventy escaped with Varro, the infantry "died nobly" to the number of seventy thousand, both sides fighting with conspicuous gallantry. Dio's fragment calls it the most fearful disaster Rome ever suffered, preceded by portents and Sibylline prophecy. Mommsen, the modern judge, treats the "unexampled success" as the moment Hannibal's grand political combination against Rome seemed to mature. Appian tallies the toll — 50,000 Roman dead in a few hours, with senators, every tribune and centurion, and both their best generals among them. Livy registers the reaction at home: never, while the city stood safe, was there so great a panic, at the loss of two consular armies and their consuls.

results.question: How do historians ancient and modern assess Pericles?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides' famous verdict: by rank, ability, and known integrity he led the multitude instead of being led, so that "what was nominally a democracy became in his hands government by the first citizen." Plutarch gives the deathbed self-assessment — his proudest claim was that "no Athenian, through my means, ever wore mourning." Grote notes that his immense ascendancy and unparalleled eloquence are attested even more forcibly by his enemies than his friends. Bury, more measured, rates him a man of finer fibre than Themistocles yet no statesman of originative genius — one who perfected the ideas of others rather than creating them.

results.question: What did ancient writers know and say about Britain and its people?
results.ideal_answer: Caesar, the first eyewitness, reports inland Britons who sow no corn, live on milk and flesh, dress in skins, dye themselves blue with woad, and hold wives in common. Tacitus weighs their origins from physical type — the ruddy-haired Caledonians suggesting German stock, the swarthy Silures Iberian. Strabo maps the island as a triangle parallel to the Gallic coast, roughly 4,300–4,400 stadia along its longest side. Cassius Dio, writing later, divides the Britons into two great races, the Caledonians and the Maeatians, who dwell coatless and shoeless in tents, hold their women in common, and rear their children as a community.

results.question: How is Cleopatra's death reported across the sources?
results.ideal_answer: All three sources report the famous asp while admitting uncertainty. Plutarch describes her found dead in royal ornaments on a golden bed, Iras dying at her feet and Charmion adjusting her diadem — but says "what really took place is known to no one," weighing the asp in the figs against poison in a hollow bodkin. Dio likewise: "no one knows clearly in what manner she perished," only slight indentations on her arm. Suetonius adds that Augustus, wanting her alive for his triumph, sent the Psylli snake-charmers to suck out the poison.

results.question: What do the sources say about Alexander's final illness and death?
results.ideal_answer: Both Plutarch and Arrian follow the Royal Diary: a fever after drinking with Medius, worsening day by day as he continued bathing and sacrificing, until he lay speechless — Arrian preserving the scene of the soldiers filing past while he could only greet them with his eyes — and died. Both record the later poisoning story; Plutarch calls it "a mere made-up story," noting the body stayed uncorrupted for days, and Arrian notes Aristobulus and Ptolemy agree with the diary, adding only the alleged last word that the kingdom would go "to the best." Diodorus, the other surviving ancient narrative, closes the twelve-year reign and reports the same poisoning rumour (Antipater's son Iollas as cup-bearer). Grote, the modern judge, weighs that rumour and concludes it was fomented by the enemies of Antipater and Cassander, above all the rancorous Olympias.

results.question: How do Roman writers describe the Germans' way of fighting?
results.ideal_answer: Caesar, from battlefield experience, describes them rapidly forming a phalanx so dense his soldiers leapt onto it to tear away shields from above. Tacitus, the ethnographer, details the framea — the short, sharp, all-purpose spear used at close quarters or thrown — the wedge-shaped battle line, the doctrine that giving ground to rally again is prudence rather than cowardice, and the carrying off of their dead mid-battle. Tacitus' Annals adds, through Germanicus' battle-speech, that the German goes without cuirass or helmet and carries only a flimsy shield of osiers or painted board. Ammianus, centuries later, shows the Alamanni in action: charging with javelins, hair streaming and eyes blazing, against the locked Roman shield-wall.

results.question: How was Caesar's funeral described, and what did it unleash?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius describes the planned pyre in the Campus Martius overtaken by the crowd: two armed men fired the bier before the Rostra and the throng heaped on benches and court furniture; afterwards the populace ran with torches to the houses of Brutus and Cassius and lynched the wrong Cinna. Plutarch adds the cause: Antony's funeral oration, climaxing in the unfolding of Caesar's bloody, pierced garment, after which the mob burned the body on an improvised pyre and ran with the half-burnt brands to fire the murderers' houses. Cassius Dio reports the same inflamed mob and the lynching of the innocent Helvius Cinna in mistake for the praetor of that name. Appian adds the decisive stagecraft: a wax effigy of Caesar revolved on a mechanical device to display all twenty-three wounds, which tipped the crowd into burning the body and the senate-house.

results.question: What did ancient writers believe about the sources and flooding of the Nile?
results.ideal_answer: Herodotus reviews three Greek theories of the summer flood — the Etesian winds, the world-encircling Ocean, and melting snow — refutes them all (offering his own sun-driven explanation), and reports that no Egyptian, Libyan, or Greek professed to know the river's sources except a Sais scribe he didn't believe. Strabo, writing centuries later, says what the ancients only conjectured had become eyewitness knowledge: the Nile owes its rise to summer rains falling in the mountains of Upper Ethiopia. Ammianus, the latest voice, rehearses the same snow-melt, Etesian-wind and Ethiopian-rain theories but judges the river's sources as unknowable to posterity as they were in his own day.

results.question: How do the sources describe the Roman siege and destruction of Jerusalem?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus, the eyewitness, gives the fullest narrative: Titus encamped about the city, and though he resolved to storm the temple, "that house, God had, for certain, long ago doomed it to the fire" — it was burned as the siege reached its terrible climax of famine and slaughter. Tacitus supplies the Roman view, describing how Titus pitched his camp before the walls and drew up his legions in order of battle against the Jews massed beneath their fortifications. Cassius Dio records the storming of the temple precinct and the city's fall — "thus was Jerusalem destroyed on the very day of Saturn, which even now the Jews reverence most."

results.question: How do the sources portray the reign and death of Herod the Great?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus gives the fullest portrait. In the Antiquities he records the grand works of the reign — in his eighteenth year Herod "undertook a very great work, that is, to build of himself the temple of God" — alongside his implacable cruelty, and then the loathsome final illness in which "his privy-member was putrefied, and produced worms," read as divine punishment. The parallel narrative in the Wars closes the reign with the bare notice that, having survived the killing of his son by only five days, Herod died, "having reigned thirty-four years."

results.question: How do the sources describe Vespasian's rise to the throne during the civil war?
results.ideal_answer: The sources agree that Vespasian was raised to the throne by the armies of the East while the Jewish war was still in progress. Josephus, present in his camp, says he "desire[d] to obtain that government, in order to corroborate his attempts upon the whole empire," and was proclaimed by the soldiers in Judaea and Egypt. Tacitus reports the news reaching the East — "messengers from Syria and Judaea brought the news that the provinces of the East had sworn allegiance to him." Suetonius records the acclamation against Vitellius: the troops in Moesia and Pannonia revolted, "as did likewise, of the armies beyond sea, those in Judaea and Syria," who swore allegiance to Vespasian as emperor.

results.question: How do the sources narrate the year of the four emperors that followed Nero?
results.ideal_answer: Tacitus gives the connected narrative, opening his Histories "with the time when Servius Galba was consul for the second time with Titus Vinius for his colleague" — the start of the convulsion that ran Galba to Otho to Vitellius to Vespasian. Plutarch's surviving lives carry the same rapid turnover: the senate, swearing in Otho's name the oath it had just sworn and broken to Galba, "conferred on him the titles of Caesar and Augustus." Suetonius' per-emperor lives record each fall in turn — Galba "perished in the seventy-third year of his age, and the seventh month of his reign," cut down in the Forum.

results.question: How do the sources assess the character and generalship of Hannibal?
results.ideal_answer: The sources agree on his extraordinary gifts while dividing on his vices. Livy's introductory sketch grants him a genius equally fit to command and obey, fearless and tireless, "by far the first of the cavalry and infantry" — but balances it with "inhuman cruelty, more than Punic perfidy." Polybius marvels at the generalship that held a polyglot army together "for sixteen continuous years" in Italy. Appian preserves the famous conversation in which Scipio asked Hannibal whom he thought the greatest general, ranking himself only behind Alexander and Pyrrhus. Mommsen, the modern judge, notes that "the Romans charged him with cruelty, the Carthaginians with covetousness," yet finds the noble image of the commander unmarred.

results.question: How do the sources describe the final destruction of Carthage?
results.ideal_answer: Appian gives the fullest narrative of 146 BC: as the city of 700 years came to its end, Scipio "is said to have shed tears and publicly lamented the fortune of the enemy," reflecting on the mutability that would one day overtake Rome itself. Polybius, his tutor and an eyewitness, records the same reflection on the fall of "the once mighty empires of the Assyrians, Medes, Persians" and Macedon, with Scipio quoting Homer on the doom of Troy. The epitome of Livy registers the bare outcome — Scipio "at length took the city by storm, in the seven hundredth year after its foundation."

results.question: How do the sources recount Rome's long wars against Mithridates of Pontus?
results.ideal_answer: Appian's dedicated narrative traces the whole struggle to its end, when Pompey brought the king's dominions "and the adjoining territory as far as the river Euphrates, under the pretext and impetus of the Mithridatic war, under Roman sway." Plutarch follows the commanders: the people "unanimously voted [Lucullus] to the Mithridatic war," which Pompey later took over. Livy's epitome records the war's terrible opening — Mithridates seized Asia, chained the proconsul and the general, "and ordered all the Romans in Asia to be massacred on the same day."

results.question: How do the sources treat the reforms and deaths of the Gracchi brothers?
results.ideal_answer: Plutarch preserves the moral force of the cause in Tiberius Gracchus's speech on the landless soldiers of Italy, who though they fought for Rome had no "houses of their own, or hearths of their ancestors to defend." Appian narrates the legislative struggle, ending when a substitute tribune was chosen "and the agrarian law was enacted." Livy's epitome summarizes the reform itself — Gracchus's agrarian law that "no person should hold more than five hundred acres of the public lands" — and the violence its passage unleashed.

results.question: How do the sources describe the war against Jugurtha of Numidia?
results.ideal_answer: The sources frame the war as much by Roman corruption as by arms. Appian preserves Jugurtha's famous verdict on the venal city — that "the whole city of Rome could be bought if a purchaser could be found for it." Plutarch follows the endgame through the Numidian kings: after Jugurtha fled and "in his distress came to [Bocchus] as his last hope," he was received as a suppliant and then betrayed. Livy's epitome records the capture plainly — Bocchus, beaten and unwilling to fight on, "delivered up Jugurtha in chains to Marius."

results.question: How do the sources tell the rise and fall of Theban supremacy under Epaminondas?
results.ideal_answer: The sources trace Theban hegemony as the personal achievement of Epaminondas — and its collapse with his death. Xenophon, contemporary and cool toward Thebes, ends his history on the inconclusive note that after Mantinea "the effective result of these achievements was the very opposite of that which the world at large anticipated." Diodorus makes the dependence explicit: while Epaminondas lived "he gained the sovereign power for his country; but, by his death they lost it again" — "and this was the end of Epaminondas, a man honoured and esteemed of all." Pausanias records his monument, whose elegiac lines tell "how he restored Messene, and how the Greeks got their freedom through him." Plutarch supplies the Theban view of the bond with Pelopidas, for whom Epaminondas "singly fought a multitude, resolving rather to die than forsake his helpless" friend.

results.question: How do the sources narrate the Athenian expedition to Sicily and its destruction?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides gives the canonical narrative of the catastrophe, ending with the survivors crammed into the Syracusan quarries, "each man during eight months having only half a pint of water and a pint of corn given him daily." Plutarch's life of the commander follows Nicias to his end, noting the dispute in the sources over how he died — that "Demosthenes and Nicias did not die, as Thucydides and Philistus have written, by the order of the Syracusans," but perhaps by their own hand.
results.notes: Diodorus XIII (#900005) also narrates this expedition but does not surface cleanly via dense retrieval (the known bad-OCR volume); Diodorus coverage is carried instead by cb-020. Thucydides + Plutarch (2 works) satisfy cross-book.

results.question: How do the sources describe the Spartan system of government attributed to Lycurgus?
results.ideal_answer: The sources credit Lycurgus with a mixed, balanced constitution. Plutarch singles out the central institution: "the first and of greatest importance was the establishment of the senate," a council of twenty-eight elders whose power, equal to the kings', acted "like ballast in a ship" between monarchy and democracy. Herodotus gives the early Greek account of the same transformation — once Lycurgus had reformed their institutions "the Spartans had good laws," and after his death "they erected a temple" to him and paid him great worship.
results.notes: Polybius VI's analysis of the Spartan constitution (in Vol. II #44126) does not surface via dense retrieval (Book VI's constitution chapters resist the query; volume is heavily Greek/sidenoted). Polybius Vol. II coverage is carried by cb-015 and cb-016. Plutarch + Herodotus (2 works) satisfy cross-book.

results.question: What do the sources report about the oracle at Delphi — its setting, procedure, and authority?
results.ideal_answer: The sources present Delphi as the most authoritative and richest of Greek shrines. Strabo records its divine charter — that "Apollo, by the aid of Themis, established this oracle with a view to benefit the human race." Pausanias preserves its ancient history, that "in the most ancient times it was the oracle of Earth" before it passed to Apollo. Diodorus shows the other side of its fame, the immense treasure that made it a target: in the Sacred War the Phocian leader's "brother and successor Onomarchus converted much of those treasures" to pay his troops, the sacrilege that defined the conflict.
results.notes: Strabo's famous cavern/vapour/tripod description (IX.3.5) is not present in this Falconer translation, which treats Delphi via its treasuries, the Sacred War, and Ephorus's rationalist account — so the spans cover the oracle's authority, history, and wealth rather than the Pythia's physical procedure.

results.question: How do the sources explain the fall of Nineveh and the end of the Assyrian empire?
results.ideal_answer: The modern reconstruction and the ancient notice agree that Nineveh fell to a coalition led by the Medes. Rawlinson's Assyria judges that the empire "fell, not so much from any inherent weakness, or from the effect of gradual decay, as by an unfortunate combination of circumstances" — a barbarian inroad coinciding with the rise of a warlike neighbour. His Media traces that neighbour's role: after the siege "the fall of the city was followed by a division of the spoil between the two principal conquerors," Cyaxares the Mede taking Assyria proper. Herodotus gives the early Greek account — the Medes "had rule over the same nations as before; and they also took Nineveh" and made the Assyrians subject, except Babylon.

results.question: What do the sources say about the walls, size, and wonders of Babylon?
results.ideal_answer: The sources concur on Babylon's vast scale. Rawlinson's modern survey calls it "probably the largest and most magnificent city of the ancient world." Herodotus describes its plan — "there are two divisions of the city; for a river whose name is Euphrates parts it in the middle" — within its enormous square of wall. Strabo gives the measurements: the wall was 385 "stadia in circumference, and 32 feet in thickness," with towers rising to 60 cubits — the defenses that, with the hanging garden, made the city one of the wonders of the world.

results.question: How do the sources describe Rome's wars against the Sassanid Persian empire?
results.ideal_answer: The sources mark the Sassanid dynasty as a renewed and formidable threat to Rome on the eastern frontier. Cassius Dio records its alarming birth — "Artaxerxes, a Persian, having conquered the Parthians in three battles and killed their king," then menaced the Roman provinces of Mesopotamia. Rawlinson's Sassanian volume traces the long struggle over the frontier fortresses, noting how Sapor "rightly conceived from the first the importance of Nisibis." Ammianus, the eyewitness of Julian's invasion, shows the Persians' method against the retreating Romans — having "learnt by their frequent defeats to shun pitched battles," they "laid secret ambuscades on our road."

results.question: How do the sources describe Rome's catastrophic defeat by the Parthians at Carrhae?
results.ideal_answer: Plutarch's life of Crassus gives the fullest narrative of the disaster — the Romans, after the battle, had "a sad night of it," unable to bury their dead or tend their wounded, "everyone bewailed his own fate." Cassius Dio supplies the end at the parley: lured to a conference by Surena, Crassus "fell among the rest, whether he was slain by one of his own men to prevent his capture alive, or whether by the enemy." Rawlinson's Parthia explains the military cause from the victors' side — to attack a cavalry power "with a body of foot-soldiers, supported by an insignificant number of horse, must be at all times rash and dangerous."

results.question: What do the sources reveal about Roman dining, banquets, and table luxury?
results.ideal_answer: The sources show both the ordinary custom and its imperial excess. Fowler describes the shape of the Roman day around its chief meal — "the dinner, cena, was in fact the principal private event of the day," coming when all business was over. Suetonius supplies the extreme of imperial gluttony, the emperor Vitellius who "always made three meals a day, sometimes four: breakfast, dinner, and supper, and a drunken revel after all," sustained by habitual vomiting. Dill, the modern voice, weighs the moralists' charges, noting that "modern criticism has laboured hard to correct some of the harsher judgments on the luxury and self-indulgence of the period of the early Empire."

results.question: How many wounds did Julius Caesar receive when he was assassinated?
results.ideal_answer: According to Suetonius, Caesar was stabbed with twenty-three wounds. He uttered a groan at the first wound but no cry, though some report that he said "And you too, my son?" to Marcus Brutus.
results.notes: Worked example for the authoring format. The wound-count (twenty-three) recurs across many works in the corpus; all are valid retrieval targets.

results.question: According to Herodotus, what did Egyptian households do when a cat died a natural death?
results.ideal_answer: According to Herodotus, everyone living in a house where a cat died a natural death shaved their eyebrows — only their eyebrows, whereas when a dog died they shaved their whole body and head.

results.question: What period of history did Gibbon declare the happiest and most prosperous for the human race?
results.ideal_answer: Gibbon wrote that if asked to fix the period during which the condition of the human race was most happy and prosperous, one would without hesitation name the span from the death of Domitian to the accession of Commodus — the era of Nerva, Trajan, Hadrian, and the Antonines.

results.question: According to Tacitus, what did the Germans consider the greatest disgrace in battle?
results.ideal_answer: Tacitus says the greatest disgrace that could befall a German warrior was to have abandoned his shield. He adds that for a chief's companions it was lifelong reproach and infamy to retreat from the field surviving their chief.
results.notes: Two famous disgrace passages — a good answer may cite either or both, so both are gold (alternatives, one requirement).

results.question: Whom did the Athenians send as a runner to Sparta before the battle of Marathon, and what happened to him on the way?
results.ideal_answer: The Athenian generals sent Pheidippides, a professional long-distance runner, as herald to Sparta. On the way, by Mount Parthenion above Tegea, the god Pan met him, called him by name, and bade him ask the Athenians why they paid him no honor although he was well disposed to them and had often been — and would again be — serviceable.
results.notes: Grote vol. IV also retells the journey itself (150 miles in 48 hours) in a separate passage without the Pan encounter — not gold (answers only half the question).

results.question: What epitaph was inscribed for the Spartans who fell at Thermopylae?
results.ideal_answer: The inscription for the Spartans read (in Macaulay's translation): "Stranger, report this word, we pray, to the Spartans, that lying here in this spot we remain, faithfully keeping their laws."

results.question: What was Augustus' boast about the city of Rome and what he did for it?
results.ideal_answer: Augustus so improved the city — which had not been built in a manner suited to the grandeur of the empire — that he boasted, in Suetonius' words, that he "found it of brick, but left it of marble."
results.notes: The saying lives in three places; any of them fully answers the question.

results.question: What honor did Caligula plan to give his horse Incitatus?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius reports it was said Caligula intended to make his horse Incitatus consul. He had already given the horse a marble stable, an ivory manger, purple housings, a jewelled frontlet, and a house with slaves and furniture for entertaining guests invited in the horse's name.

results.question: How does Caesar describe the division of Gaul at the start of his commentaries?
results.ideal_answer: Caesar opens by stating that all Gaul is divided into three parts, one inhabited by the Belgae, another by the Aquitani, and the third by the people called Celts in their own language and Gauls in Latin — all differing from each other in language, customs, and laws.

results.question: What answer did the Delphic oracle give Croesus when he asked whether to attack Persia?
results.ideal_answer: The oracles answered that if Croesus marched against the Persians he would destroy a great empire, and counselled him to find the most powerful of the Hellenes and make them his friends. The prophecy was ambiguous — the great empire he destroyed turned out to be his own.

results.question: What were Nero's last words about himself as he prepared to die?
results.ideal_answer: As his grave was prepared before his eyes, Nero wept and repeatedly said, "What an artist is now about to perish!"

results.question: How many Jews perished and how many were taken captive during the whole siege of Jerusalem in the Jewish War?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus reports that the number carried captive during the whole war was ninety-seven thousand, and the number who perished during the whole siege was eleven hundred thousand (1,100,000) — most of them Jews who had come up from the country to Jerusalem for the feast of unleavened bread and were then shut in by the Roman army, destroyed by pestilence and famine.

results.question: What did Eleazar urge the defenders of Masada to do rather than fall into Roman hands?
results.ideal_answer: Eleazar urged the defenders to die free by their own hands rather than be taken — to slay their own wives and children and then one another, having first burned their possessions and the fortress, so as to preserve their freedom and deny the Romans the pleasure of capturing them. They carried out the mass suicide, sparing only their stores of food.

results.question: What act of a starving mother during the famine in Jerusalem does Josephus describe in the Wars of the Jews?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus describes Mary of Bethezuba, a once-wealthy woman driven to madness by the famine, who killed her infant son, roasted him, ate one half and kept the other half concealed — an atrocity he says had no parallel in Greek or barbarian history.

results.question: By what act could a man become the priest of Diana's grove at Nemi, and what was the priest called?
results.ideal_answer: A man could win the priesthood only by breaking off a bough of a certain sacred tree in the grove and then slaying the reigning priest in single combat; having killed him, he succeeded to the office and held it until he in turn was slain by a stronger or craftier challenger. The priest bore the title King of the Wood (Rex Nemorensis).

results.question: What was the supreme or national god of the Assyrians?
results.ideal_answer: The supreme, distinctively national god of the Assyrians was Asshur — head of the Assyrian pantheon, styled "the great Lord" and "King of all the Gods," the tutelary deity of the kings and of the country, whose name was identical with that of Assyria itself.

results.question: What was the principal building material of Chaldaea, and why was stone so little used?
results.ideal_answer: The principal building material of Chaldaea was brick, made from the abundant clay of the alluvial plain (sun-dried, or kiln-baked for greater hardness). Stone was scarcely used because the alluvium was destitute of it — any stone had to be imported from adjacent countries — so the Chaldaeans built with clay brick in its default.

results.question: How many men were appointed to draw up the laws at Athens and became known as the Thirty?
results.ideal_answer: Thirty men were chosen — the body that became known as the Thirty (later the Thirty Tyrants) — appointed to draft a constitution based on the ancestral laws of Athens, though they never published the laws and instead seized power for themselves.

results.question: What did Maharbal say to Hannibal after the battle of Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: When Hannibal hesitated to march at once on Rome after Cannae, his cavalry commander Maharbal — who had urged that within five days Hannibal might dine in the Capitol — rebuked him: "You know how to conquer, Hannibal, but you do not know how to make use of your victory." Livy adds that this day's delay is believed to have saved the city and the empire.

results.question: What trophy of gold rings was poured out before the Carthaginian senate to show the scale of the victory at Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: To prove the scale of the victory, Mago had the gold rings stripped from the Roman knights slain at Cannae poured out in the vestibule of the Carthaginian senate-house — a heap that, by report, filled about a peck (some claimed three and a half pecks) — explaining that only knights, and of these the principal only, wore such rings.

results.question: What did Scipio Africanus say to the crowd on the anniversary of his victory when prosecuted, before leading them to the Capitol?
results.ideal_answer: Brought to trial on the very anniversary of the battle, Scipio reminded the assembly that on that day he had fought and beaten Hannibal and the Carthaginians in Africa (at Zama); declaring it unfit to spend such a day in litigation, he went up to the Capitol to thank the gods — and the whole assembly left the tribunes and followed him.

results.question: Which famous maxims of the sages were inscribed in the forecourt of the temple of Apollo at Delphi?
results.ideal_answer: The two famous maxims inscribed by the Seven Wise Men in the vestibule (forecourt) of the temple of Apollo at Delphi were "Know thyself" and "Nothing in excess" — the latter rendered by Pausanias as "Not too much of anything."

results.question: How did a Roman patron's clients begin his day, in the morning ceremony at his house?
results.ideal_answer: A patron's clients began his day with the salutatio — the morning reception. In the early hours after daybreak, clients and callers gathered at his house to greet him and pay their respects before he went down to the Forum, often escorting him there in a train; under the Empire they thronged the reception hoping for an invitation to dinner or a dole (the sportula).
results.notes: Two attestations of the morning reception (salutatio) — Fowler for the Republic, Dill for the Empire — are alternatives; either fully answers.

results.question: What city did Alexander found in memory of the horse he had tamed as a boy?
results.ideal_answer: Bucephala, on the Hydaspes. As a boy Alexander tamed the horse Bucephalas (turning him toward the sun after noticing he feared his own shadow); when the horse died of age and toil after the battle with Porus, Alexander founded the city of Bucephala in his memory.

results.question: How did the wife of the emperor who added Britain to the empire meet her end?
results.ideal_answer: The emperor is Claudius, who invaded Britain to gain a triumph; his wife Messalina, after publicly marrying her lover Silius, was ordered to answer for her conduct, failed in her attempts at suicide in the gardens of Lucullus, and was run through with a sword by a tribune.

results.question: Who tutored the son of the king who crushed the Greeks at Chaeronea?
results.ideal_answer: Aristotle. The king is Philip of Macedon, victor at Chaeronea (where his son Alexander led the charge against the Theban Sacred Band); Philip had earlier sent for Aristotle, the most celebrated philosopher of the age, to instruct Alexander, rewarding him by rebuilding his native Stagira.

results.question: Who avenged the Roman commander killed at Carrhae?
results.ideal_answer: Ventidius (Publius Ventidius Bassus), Antony's lieutenant. The commander killed at Carrhae was Crassus, slain at a treacherous parley after the defeat by the Parthians; Ventidius later defeated the Parthians in three battles, killing prince Pacorus, a victory Plutarch says "fully avenged" the defeats under Crassus.

results.question: Why did the Greek army Xenophon served with have to fight its way home?
results.ideal_answer: Their employer Cyrus the Younger was killed at Cunaxa charging his brother King Artaxerxes (struck by a javelin under the eye), leaving the Greeks victorious but stranded deep in Persia; then Tissaphernes treacherously seized their five generals at a conference and cut down the captains, leaving the Ten Thousand leaderless and forced to fight their way to the sea.

results.question: How did Alexander react on finding the tomb of the founder of the Persian empire desecrated?
results.ideal_answer: Finding the tomb of Cyrus (whose epitaph read "I am Cyrus, son of Cambyses, who founded the empire of the Persians") dug through and pillaged at Pasargadae, Alexander was grieved; he commissioned Aristobulus to restore the tomb and its furnishings, seal the entrance, and he had the Magian guards arrested and tortured to find the culprits.
results.notes: All hops within Arrian's long tomb chapter, but spread across multiple chunks at our chunk size — still a genuine multi-chunk retrieval test. Now also cross-source (Strabo, Plutarch, Grote) after the new ingest.

results.question: Which Spartan commanded the Greeks at the land battle fought the same day as the sea battle at Mycale?
results.ideal_answer: Pausanias, son of Cleombrotos (regent for Leonidas' young son Pleistarchos). Herodotus reports that the victory at Plataea and the fight at Mycale fell on the same day, and that Pausanias was appointed to lead the Spartan army that won at Plataea.
results.notes: Caveat: IX.101 ("a victory had been already gained by the Hellenes with Pausanias") nearly answers both hops in one passage — the hop-1 span sits adjacent to it. Grote vol. V DOES carry a single chunk answering both hops (added above); the new ingest also brings Bury, Pausanias the geographer, and Rawlinson into play.

results.question: How did the statesman who championed Athens' Long Walls die?
results.ideal_answer: Pericles — who, as Socrates attested, proposed the long wall to the people — died of the plague of Athens, in his case not with violent fits but as a dull, lingering distemper that slowly wasted his body and mind.

results.question: How did the two leading assassins of Caesar die?
results.ideal_answer: Both died at Philippi. Cassius, mistakenly believing the battle lost, bared his neck to his freedman Pindarus and was beheaded; in the second battle Brutus, defeated, fell on his sword — held, by some accounts, by his friend Strato — and died.

results.question: What revolt broke out in Britain under the emperor who was the last of his dynasty?
results.ideal_answer: The revolt of Boudicca (Buduica), queen of the Iceni, broke out under Nero — whom Dio calls the last of the descendants of Aeneas and Augustus. While the governor Paulinus was away at Mona she sacked two Roman cities with enormous slaughter before being defeated.

results.question: How did the brother of Hannibal who marched an army into Italy to reinforce him meet his end?
results.ideal_answer: Hasdrubal, Hannibal's brother, crossed the Alps into Italy to reinforce him but was defeated and killed by the consuls Marcus Livius and Claudius Nero at the river Metaurus (207 BC); his severed head, carried south, was thrown before the outposts of Hannibal's camp.
results.notes: All spans in Livy Books 27-36 (#12582), which was ZERO. The combined span is Livy's book-argument header (legitimate canonical text, like the Periochae spans elsewhere) and covers both hops at once.

results.question: How did the Carthaginian commander who led elephants across the Alps eventually die, long after his war with Rome?
results.ideal_answer: Hannibal, who led his army with its elephants across the Alps into Italy in the Second Punic War, died decades later in exile: cornered at the court of king Prusias by a Roman demand for his surrender, he drank the poison he had long kept ready.
results.notes: hop2 spans overlap con-014 (which frames the manner-of-death as a contradiction); here the question is identify-then-fate. Routes Livy 37-end #44318 (thin) and Appian #900003.

results.question: Who commanded the Roman army that razed Carthage to the ground, and what did he reportedly weep over as the city burned?
results.ideal_answer: Scipio Aemilianus (Scipio the Younger), who destroyed Carthage in the Third Punic War (146 BC). As the city burned he shed tears and, asked by Polybius what he meant, admitted he frankly named his own country — fearing that one day the same fate would befall Rome.
results.notes: The famous Homeric verse Scipio is said to have quoted is a LACUNA in our Appian text (it jumps from "splendid Macedonian" straight to "Being asked by Polybius"), so the ideal answer claims only what the corpus attests — the weeping and his fear for Rome — not the Homer quotation. Both hop2 spans sit in one Appian chunk. Routes Appian #900003 + Livy epitomes #44318.

results.question: Which Theban general broke Spartan power in a great pitched battle, and where did he fall in his last victory?
results.ideal_answer: Epaminondas of Thebes, who shattered Spartan supremacy at Leuctra (371 BC) and fell mortally wounded at Mantinea (362 BC), dying in the very hour of his victory.
results.notes: Fills Xenophon Hellenica #1174 (Leuctra) and Pausanias Vol. II #68680; routes Diodorus #900005.

results.question: Which Achaean coastal city was swallowed by the sea in a single night, and what was blamed for the catastrophe?
results.ideal_answer: Helike (Helice) in Achaea, which an earthquake and an inundation of the sea overwhelmed in a single night (373 BC). The catastrophe was blamed on the wrath of Poseidon, whose suppliants the people of Helike had dragged from his temple and slain.
results.notes: The two Pausanias spans sit in adjacent/overlapping chunks (submersion vs. divine cause); the Diodorus alternate adds genuine cross-source distance for hop1. Fills Pausanias Vol. II #68680, routes Diodorus #900005.

results.question: How did the Spartan regent who led the Greeks to victory at Plataea fall from power and die?
results.ideal_answer: Pausanias the regent, who commanded the victorious Greeks at Plataea (479 BC), afterwards intrigued with Persia to make himself master of Greece; detected, he fled to the temple of Athena of the Brazen House, where the ephors roofed him in, barricaded the doors and starved him, bringing him out only as he was about to expire.
results.notes: hop1 overlaps mh-007 (Pausanias as the Plataea commander) — a different question (here his downfall). The candidate listed Diodorus XI (#900005) for hop2, but #900005 contains only the unrelated Pausanias who murdered Philip (Book XVI), not the regent's death — so Diodorus was dropped here.

results.question: The empire whose supreme god was Asshur fell when its great capital was stormed — who destroyed that capital?
results.ideal_answer: Assyria — whose national god was Asshur and whose capital was Nineveh — fell when Nineveh was stormed by the Medes under Cyaxares allied with the Babylonians under Nabopolassar; the last Assyrian king, his means of resistance exhausted, burned himself in his palace.
results.notes: The two hop1 spans are complementary attestations (Asshur = Assyria's god; Nineveh = its capital) grouped as alternatives — either one bridges the riddle to Assyria/Nineveh. hop2 spans reused from con-012. Routes Rawlinson Assyria #16162 (mostly ZERO).

results.question: How did the founder of the Persian empire meet his death on campaign?
results.ideal_answer: Cyrus the Great, founder of the Persian empire, was killed on campaign beyond the Araxes against the Massagetae and their queen Tomyris; the greater part of his army was destroyed and Cyrus himself fell there, after a reign of twenty-nine years.
results.notes: Routes Rawlinson Persia #16165 / Media #16163 on hop1. Rawlinson's narrative of Cyrus's death by the Massagetae did not surface cleanly, so hop2 rests on Herodotus I.214.

results.question: Which Jewish prisoner predicted that his Roman captor would become emperor, and did the prophecy come true?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus, captured at Jotapata, predicted to Vespasian that he would become Caesar and emperor. The prophecy came true when the eastern legions proclaimed Vespasian emperor (AD 69) — Tiberius Alexander first swearing the legions of Egypt to him on 1 July — and Vespasian, recalling the prophecy, freed Josephus.
results.notes: Fills Josephus Wars #2850 and Tacitus Histories #900002.

results.question: Which king rebuilt the Jerusalem temple on a grand scale, and how did he die?
results.ideal_answer: Herod the Great, who rebuilt the Temple at Jerusalem on a magnificent scale, died of a loathsome wasting disease — fever, ulcerated entrails, dropsy, and putrefying flesh that bred worms — shortly after putting his son Antipater to death.
results.notes: Fills Josephus Antiquities #2848 (rebuild + death) and routes Wars #2850. Antiquities ("eighteenth year") and Wars ("fifteenth year") differ on the regnal year — both attest the rebuild.

results.question: How did the reign of the emperor Vitellius come to its end?
results.ideal_answer: Vitellius's army was crushed by the Flavian forces at the second battle of Cremona (Bedriacum); when Rome fell, Vitellius was dragged from hiding through the streets to the Gemonian stairs, tortured and killed, and his body cast into the Tiber.
results.notes: Fills Tacitus Histories #900002.

results.question: After the catastrophe at Cannae, what feature of Rome's system of government was credited with explaining her swift recovery?
results.ideal_answer: Rome's mixed, balanced constitution — the three elements of consuls (monarchic), senate (aristocratic) and people (democratic), each checking the others — which Polybius describes precisely at the epoch of Cannae as the source of Roman stability and resilience.
results.notes: CORRECTION to the candidate: Polybius Book VI sits in Shuckburgh Vol. I (#44125), NOT Vol. II (#44126). So this question routes #44125 (already non-ZERO) and does NOT fill the #44126 ZERO gap the candidate assumed; the later/fragmentary books in #44126 remain uncovered by these additions.

results.question: Which oracle did Croesus famously test before his war with Persia, and what natural phenomenon there was later said to produce its prophecies?
results.ideal_answer: The oracle at Delphi, which Croesus tested (by the secret boiled-tortoise riddle) and judged the only true oracle before consulting it about war with Persia. Its prophecies were later attributed to an exhalation rising from a cleft in the earth, which inspired the Pythian priestess to her divine frenzy.
results.notes: Fills Strabo Vol. II #44885 (vol-split caveat confirmed: Strabo Book IX is in Vol. II). Overlaps syn-str-01 on the vapour fact; here it is a multi-hop crossed with Herodotus's account of Croesus testing the oracle.

results.question: What naval catastrophe destroyed the great Athenian expedition to Sicily, and what became of its captured general?
results.ideal_answer: The Athenian armament was annihilated at Syracuse — beaten decisively in the Great Harbour and then destroyed "with a total destruction" in the retreat (413 BC). The captured general Nicias was put to death by the Syracusans (Thucydides), though Plutarch preserves a variant that he and Demosthenes were instead allowed to take their own lives.
results.notes: Overlaps con-019 on Nicias's fate (hop2 spans shared); here it is identify-then-fate. The Diodorus XIII narrative of the Sicilian disaster is too OCR-garbled in #900005 to quote reliably, so this routes Thucydides #7142 + Plutarch #674 rather than Diodorus.

results.question: What were the symptoms of the epidemic that devastated Athens?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides (who had the disease himself) describes the "distemper" thus: people in good health were suddenly attacked by violent heats in the head, redness and inflammation of the eyes, a bloody throat and tongue with fetid breath, then sneezing, hoarseness, chest pain and a hard cough, stomach upset and retching with violent spasms. Most died around the seventh to ninth day; in survivors the disease could descend into the bowels (fatal diarrhoea), or cost them fingers, toes, eyes, or memory.
results.notes: Vocabulary gap — "epidemic" never appears; the texts say distemper/pestilence/malady. Swept new corpus 2026-06-13: Bury (900006) and Diodorus (900005) both retell the plague but neither lists the SYMPTOMS the question asks for (Bury covers origin/social collapse, Diodorus is brief) — not gold spans for this question.

results.question: What role did mass kidnapping play in early Rome's search for wives?
results.ideal_answer: Rome under Romulus was short of women and neighboring peoples refused intermarriage, so Romulus staged games (the Consualia) and invited the neighbors; at a signal the Roman youths rushed in and carried off the unmarried women — the "rape of the Sabine women." The ensuing war with the Sabines ended with the women themselves brokering peace and the two peoples merging.

results.question: How did the Sabine commander Mettius Curtius end up in a swamp during the battle for Rome?
results.ideal_answer: During the battle between Romans and Sabines on the future site of the Forum, Mettius Curtius' horse took fright at the noise of his pursuers and carried him headlong into a morass; he barely escaped (Plutarch says he abandoned the mired horse and saved himself). The spot was afterwards called the Curtian Lake.

results.question: What omens were believed to have foretold Claudius' death?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius lists as the chief presages a comet, lightning striking his father Drusus' monument, and the deaths of magistrates of all ranks that year. Cassius Dio adds a shower of blood, a thunderbolt on the Praetorian standards, the temple of Jupiter Victor opening of its own accord, and bees swarming in the camp.

results.question: Does Thucydides describe anything like a tsunami?
results.ideal_answer: Yes. He reports that during the earthquakes of 426 BC the sea at Orobiae in Euboea retired from the coast and returned in a huge wave, flooding part of the town and leaving some of it permanently under water, killing those who could not reach high ground (with a similar inundation at Atalanta). He even gives the correct causal explanation: the earthquake drives the sea back, and it recoils with redoubled force.
results.notes: Swept new corpus 2026-06-13: Diodorus' vivid tsunami (Helice & Bura, 900005) is the 373 BC Achaean quake — a DIFFERENT event, not Thucydides' 426 BC Orobiae/Atalanta wave — so not a gold span here. GROUPING JUDGMENT (review): the two spans (the wave + its cause) sit in one chapter and either alone answers "does he describe a tsunami?", so they are one requirement [tsunami]. Split into two requirements if you want recall to demand the causal-explanation passage as well.

results.question: Which Persian king was said to suffer from epilepsy from birth?
results.ideal_answer: Cambyses. Herodotus reports that he was said to have suffered from birth from a grievous malady "called by some the sacred disease" (the ancient name for epilepsy), and remarks it was no surprise that with so diseased a body his mind was not sound either.
results.notes: Swept Grote vol. IV — he describes Cambyses' madness but not the congenital disease, so not a gold span. Swept Rawlinson's Persia (16165, new corpus 2026-06-13): he argues the madness was an Egyptian-priest fiction and omits the sacred disease entirely — not a gold span either.

results.question: How were the pontoon bridges across the Hellespont built?
results.ideal_answer: After a storm destroyed the first pair (built by Phoenicians with flax cables and Egyptians with papyrus cables), the rebuilt bridges rested on rows of anchored fifty-oared galleys and triremes — 360 under one bridge and 314 under the other — over which cables of white flax and papyrus were stretched tight with wooden windlasses; planks, brushwood, and stamped earth were laid on top, with barriers so the animals would not see the sea.

results.question: What solar eclipse marked the departure of Xerxes' army, and how was it interpreted?
results.ideal_answer: As the army set out from Sardis in spring, the sun left its place in a perfectly clear sky and day became night. Xerxes, concerned, asked the Magians, who declared the god was foreshowing to the Greeks the abandonment of their cities — since the sun was the foreshower of events for the Greeks, the moon for the Persians. Xerxes marched on greatly pleased.

results.question: How did the Persian royal postal relay system work?
results.ideal_answer: According to Herodotus, men and horses were stationed along the route, one for each day's journey; the message was carried by the first rider to the second, the second to the third, and so on, like the Greek torch-race. Neither snow, rain, heat, nor darkness of night stopped them — nothing mortal travelled faster. The Persians called it the angareion.

results.question: Which people beyond Scythia practiced cannibalism?
results.ideal_answer: The Androphagoi ("man-eaters"). Herodotus describes them as nomads dwelling north of Scythia with their own language and the most savage manners of all human beings, acknowledging no law — and alone of all these nations they are man-eaters.

results.question: What celibate Jewish brotherhood that held all property in common and despised private wealth is described at length?
results.ideal_answer: The Essenes. Josephus devotes long passages to this Jewish sect: they reject pleasure, neglect marriage, hold riches in contempt, and own nothing privately — by their law all property is thrown into a common stock, "so that a rich man enjoys no more of his own wealth than he who hath nothing at all," with about four thousand living this way.
results.notes: Vocab gap — question's "celibate/commune/brotherhood/private wealth" never appears; the texts say Essens/order/common. Fills the two ZERO-coverage Josephus volumes (Wars #2850, Antiquities #2848).

results.question: What stepped, multi-tiered temple-tower did the Babylonians raise at the center of their cities?
results.ideal_answer: The ziggurat — a solid temple-tower built in receding stages (square set upon square), the most essential feature of a Babylonian temple, with a shrine at its summit. The number of stages ranged from two up to seven or eight; the great temple of Belus at Babylon was the famous example.
results.notes: Modern "ziggurat" vs archaic "temple-tower"/"tower in stages". NOTE the Rawlinson span itself uses the word "ziggurat", so the vocab gap is carried mainly by the Herodotus alternative. Fills Rawlinson Babylon (#16164).

results.question: What surprise attack on the beached Athenian fleet brought the long war between Athens and Sparta to an end?
results.ideal_answer: The battle of Aegospotami (405 BC). With the Athenian crews scattered ashore in search of food, Lysander rowed across the Hellespont and caught the fleet drawn up on the open beach; all but a handful of ships (Conon escaped with nine) were taken without a fight. The loss of the fleet left Athens unable to continue the Peloponnesian War, and she surrendered the next year.
results.notes: Vocab gap — "surprise attack/fleet" vs the texts' ships drawn up on shore with dispersed crews. Fills the ZERO-coverage Xenophon Hellenica (#1174).

results.question: How were the victorious Athenian commanders punished for failing to rescue survivors after the sea battle at Arginusae?
results.ideal_answer: After winning at Arginusae (406 BC) the generals were accused of failing to pick up the shipwrecked crews (they blamed a storm). The Athenian assembly, illegally trying them collectively rather than one by one, condemned the eight; the six who had returned to Athens were executed. The city soon repented of the act.
results.notes: Vocab gap — modern "court-martialled/executed" vs the texts' tried/condemned/ put to death. Fills Xenophon Hellenica (#1174).

results.question: How was a Roman army's overnight fortified camp laid out, with its regular streets and gates?
results.ideal_answer: On a fixed, repeatable plan. The general's tent (praetorium) was set first, then the lines of the tribunes' and legions' quarters measured off from it; the whole camp formed a square crossed by regular streets (viae) "like a town", with four gates, a market-place, and a broad clear space inside the rampart — so familiar that each soldier could find his quarters as in his own city.
results.notes: Vocab gap — modern "grid layout/standardized plan" vs the texts' encampment/ streets/gates. COVERAGE CAVEAT — the candidate file targeted Polybius Vol. II (#44126) to fill its ZERO coverage, but Polybius's camp-layout description (Book VI) actually sits in Vol. I (#44125), already covered. This question therefore does NOT fill the #44126 gap; spans point to where the answer really lives (Polybius I + Josephus + Gibbon).

results.question: What strategy of refusing open battle and wearing the invader down by delay was used against Hannibal in Italy?
results.ideal_answer: The cautious, delaying strategy of the dictator Quintus Fabius Maximus, who shadowed Hannibal from the heights, cut off his supplies and refused a pitched battle — earning the surname Cunctator ("the Delayer"/"Lingerer") and, at first, scorn at Rome, before it was recognised as the policy that saved the state.
results.notes: Vocab gap — modern "attrition/delaying tactics" vs cunctator/protracting/ procrastination. Fills the thin Livy 9-26 (#10907).

results.question: How did Hannibal's men break apart the rock blocking a pass while crossing the Alps?
results.ideal_answer: They split it by fire and vinegar: piling and burning timber against the rock to heat it, then pouring vinegar on the heated stone to make it soft and crumbling, after which they cut a way through with iron tools.
results.notes: Vocab gap — modern "dissolve/split the rock" vs heated/poured vinegar/ crumbling. Fills the thin Livy 9-26 (#10907); three independent works.

results.question: What secret nocturnal religious society did the Roman senate outlaw as a criminal conspiracy?
results.ideal_answer: The Bacchanalia — the secret night-rites of Bacchus. After they spread from Etruria through Italy and into Rome, the senate in 186 BC treated them as a dangerous conspiracy: thousands were tried and many executed, the meeting places demolished, and a decree forbade the rites in Rome or Italy except under strict, tiny, senate-licensed exceptions.
results.notes: Vocab gap — modern "cult/outlaw/conspiracy" vs Bacchanalia/nocturnal rites (though Livy's own narrative does use "conspiracy"). Fills the thin Livy 37-end (#44318).

results.question: What intoxicating vapor rising from a cleft in the ground was believed to put the priestess at Delphi into a prophetic frenzy?
results.ideal_answer: An exhalation rising from a deep cleft or cavern at Delphi. The Pythian priestess sat on a tripod set over the opening, breathed in the rising exhalation, and, seized by a divine frenzy, delivered the oracle's response.
results.notes: Vocab gap — "exhalation"/"inspiration"/"enthusiasm" in the texts. NB this corrects cb-023's note (which claimed Strabo's vapour/tripod description is absent from the Falconer translation — it is in fact present at IX.3.5). Fills the ZERO-coverage Strabo Vol. II (#44885).

results.question: What Greek coastal city was swallowed by the sea after an earthquake, leaving its ruins visible beneath the water?
results.ideal_answer: Helike, in Achaea. One winter night (373 BC) a violent earthquake struck and the sea simultaneously rushed in, submerging the city and its people; for a long time afterwards its ruins could be seen under the water, growing fainter as the sea wore them away.
results.notes: Vocab gap — modern "tsunami/submerged" vs swallowed up/overwhelmed by the waves. Fills the ZERO-coverage Pausanias Vol. II (#68680). (Diodorus #900005 also narrates Helike & Bura but is the known bad-OCR volume — its garbled text does not resolve cleanly, so it is not a span here.)

results.question: What great temple on the Capitol was burned down during the street fighting in Rome in the civil war of AD 69?
results.ideal_answer: The temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus (the Capitol) was burned to the ground during the fighting between the Vitellians and the supporters of Vespasian. Tacitus calls it the most deplorable disgrace to befall Rome since the city's foundation — the seat of Jupiter, untouched by Porsenna or the Gauls, openly besieged and set on fire.
results.notes: Vocab gap — modern "burned down/arson" vs set on fire/conflagration. Single span — the AD 69 burning is Tacitus's set-piece; fills the ZERO-coverage Tacitus Histories (#900002). (Appian #900004's "the Capitol was burned" is the 83 BC Sullan fire — a different event — so not a span here.)

results.question: How did the soldiers turn against the emperor Galba and kill him in the forum?
results.ideal_answer: Bought over by Otho's promises, the praetorians abandoned Galba; as he was carried into the Forum his bearers panicked and tipped him from his litter near the Lacus Curtius, where the soldiers cut him down — most reports saying a legionary named Camurius severed his throat — and then beheaded and mutilated the old emperor, consul and high priest, before the senators and crowd.
results.notes: Vocab gap — modern "coup/assassinate" vs slew/struck/murdered. Fills the ZERO-coverage Tacitus Histories (#900002).

results.question: On what furniture did well-to-do Romans recline while eating the main meal of the day?
results.ideal_answer: They reclined on couches (lecti) rather than sitting. The cena (dinner) was the chief meal of the day, and after the Punic wars special dining rooms — the triclinia, named for the three couches arranged around the table — were built for the comfort of reclining guests, with no more than three to a couch as good manners.
results.notes: Vocab gap — modern "dinner-party seating/furniture" vs couches/lectus/ triclinium/reclining. Fills the ZERO-coverage Fowler (#11256). Both spans are within the one target work (alternatives).

results.question: How does Plutarch portray Antony's ruin through Cleopatra?
results.ideal_answer: Plutarch frames Cleopatra as "the last and crowning mischief" that befell Antony — a love that awakened his dormant passions and corrupted what goodness and judgment remained. She kept him captive through ceaseless charm (dicing, drinking, hunting with him, never letting him escape by day or night); the passion rekindled "like Plato's restive horse" when he returned east and showered her with provinces; and after Actium it left him a misanthrope playing Timon of Athens in a sea-hut, before the final catastrophe in Alexandria.

results.question: How does Arrian portray Alexander's bond with his soldiers?
results.ideal_answer: As intense mutual devotion that survived even open conflict. At the Hyphasis the army's silent, grieved resistance forced the invincible king to turn back; when he nearly died among the Malli, the troops wept, cheered, and crowded to touch his hands, knees, and clothes as he showed himself alive; and at Opis the mutiny dissolved when he called every Macedonian his kinsman, sealing the reconciliation with kisses and a vast common banquet.

results.question: How does Livy portray the struggle between patricians and plebeians?
results.ideal_answer: As the defining, recurring affliction of the early Republic — "the discord among the several orders... the bane of this city." Driven by debt and oppression, the commons twice seceded (the first ended by Menenius Agrippa's fable of the belly and the members, the second on the Sacred Mount against the decemvirs); each crisis extracted new concessions — tribunes, appeal, laws — while Livy moralizes on how hard moderation is when each side's pursuit of power or liberty makes it an object of dread to the other.

results.question: How did the Romans become a naval power during the First Punic War, according to Polybius?
results.ideal_answer: Through what Polybius calls extraordinary audacity. Having never thought of naval war and lacking even the knowledge to build quinqueremes, they used a wrecked Carthaginian ship as their model, trained rowers on benches set up on dry land, and invented the "crow" boarding-bridge that turned sea battles into land fights (first proven at Mylae); after catastrophic storm losses they persisted, rebuilt with lighter ships and trained crews, and won the decisive battle of Aegusa.

results.question: How does Thucydides describe the moral collapse caused by civil war?
results.ideal_answer: In his analysis of the Corcyra revolution, Thucydides argues that war is "a rough master" that levels men's characters to their fortunes. Words lost their ordinary meanings (recklessness became courage, moderation cowardice); religion was honored by neither side while fair phrases served guilty ends; the moderates perished between the factions; the "ancient simplicity" of honor was laughed out of existence and no man trusted his fellow. The concrete horror at Corcyra — fathers killing sons, suppliants slain at altars — set the pattern the whole Hellenic world then exceeded.

results.question: How does Thucydides contrast the Athenian and Spartan national characters?
results.ideal_answer: Largely through speeches. The Corinthians tell Sparta that the Athenians are addicted to innovation, swift in conception and execution, adventurous beyond their power, never at home — "born into the world to take no rest themselves and to give none to others" — while the Spartans keep what they have, invent nothing, and never go far enough. Archidamus answers by defending Spartan slowness as disciplined wisdom: warlike from self-control and honor, wise because educated too little to despise the laws.

results.question: What does Herodotus find most remarkable about Babylon?
results.ideal_answer: Its unmatched magnificence and its customs. He marvels at the city's scale — the deep moat and walls fifty royal cubits thick and two hundred high, and the temple of Zeus Belos with its eight stacked towers — and singles out two customs: the annual bride auction, where money from the beautiful maidens dowered the plain ones (in his opinion their wisest), and the carrying of the sick into the marketplace, since they use no physicians and every passer-by must offer advice.

results.question: How does Suetonius chart Caligula's descent from popular prince to monster?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius structures the Life around an explicit pivot: "Thus far we have spoken of him as a prince. What remains to be said of him, bespeaks him rather a monster than a man." His accession brought genuine joy — the soldiers' beloved camp-child, heir to Germanicus' virtues — though Tiberius had already predicted he was "rearing a hydra for the people of Rome." After the pivot comes the monster: claiming divine majesty, beheading the gods' statues to mount his own head, the temple to his own divinity, and cruelties without limit.

results.question: How does Tacitus characterize German marriage and the position of women?
results.ideal_answer: With pointed admiration. The marriage gifts — oxen, a bridled horse, shield, spear, and sword — admonish the wife that she comes as a partner in toils and dangers, "to suffer and to dare equally with him, in peace and in war... thus she is to live; thus to die." The Germans live "fenced around with chastity": adultery is extremely rare and instantly punished, no one calls seduction the way of the world, and in the strictest tribes a woman takes one husband "as one body and one life."

results.question: How does Suetonius portray the character of Tiberius?
results.ideal_answer: As a dissimulating tyrant whose vices, long imperfectly concealed, flowered once he withdrew to Capri. Even Augustus reportedly pitied the Roman people for being "ground by the jaws of such a slow devourer." The soldiers' nickname Biberius marked his drunkenness from youth; at Capri, "remote from the observation of the people of Rome, he abandoned himself to all the vicious propensities which he had long but imperfectly concealed"; and the translator's verdict is of an "execrable tyrant" whose indiscriminate cruelty spared neither age nor sex.

results.question: How does Josephus portray the factional strife among the Jews that doomed Jerusalem?
